title: "Data science walkthrough 1"

  revealjs:
    toc: false
execute:
  echo: true

  warning: false
  message: false
---




## An example research question

> Since 2000, did the country in Asia with the lowest MCV1 coverage also have the highest measles incidence per capita?

## The programming idea

- This is something that would be pretty easy to do by hand (e.g. with Excel), since the dataset is not all that big.
- The difficult part for programming is **translating this question into computer code.**
- The computer will always do exactly what we tell it to, so we have to turn the steps to solving this question into specific computer code.

## Steps to solve this problem

1. Get the dataset
1. Separate out countries in Asia and years since 2000
1. Calculate per capita incidence
1. Find the country with the lowest MCV1 coverage
1. Find the country with the highest per capita incidence
1. Are they the same?

## Load the data




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
data_file <- here::here("data", "measles_final.Rds")

measles <- readr::read_rds(data_file)
```
:::




## Look at the data




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
str(measles)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
spc_tbl_ [9,416 × 9] (S3: spec_tbl_df/tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
 $ iso3c        : chr [1:9416] "AFG" "AFG" "AFG" "AFG" ...
 $ year         : num [1:9416] 2023 2022 2021 2020 2019 ...
 $ measles_cases: num [1:9416] 2792 5166 2900 640 353 ...
 $ population   : num [1:9416] 42239854 41128771 40099462 38972230 37769499 ...
 $ MCV1_coverage: num [1:9416] NA 68 63 66 64 71 67 64 62 60 ...
 $ MCV2_coverage: num [1:9416] NA 49 44 43 41 49 40 40 42 44 ...
 $ country      : chr [1:9416] "Afghanistan" "Afghanistan" "Afghanistan" "Afghanistan" ...
 $ region       : chr [1:9416] "Asia" "Asia" "Asia" "Asia" ...
 $ sub_region   : chr [1:9416] "Southern Asia" "Southern Asia" "Southern Asia" "Southern Asia" ...
 - attr(*, "spec")=
  .. cols(
  ..   iso3c = col_character(),
  ..   year = col_double(),
  ..   measles_cases = col_double(),
  ..   population = col_double(),
  ..   MCV1_coverage = col_double(),
  ..   MCV2_coverage = col_double()
  .. )
 - attr(*, "problems")=<pointer: (nil)> 
```


:::
:::




## What do we need?

For this question, we need:

- `country`, because we need to compare the countries in the two results
- `region`, because we only want countries in Asia
- `year`, because we only want 2000 and later
- `measles_cases`, because we need incidence
- `population`, because raw case counts are hard to compare
- `MCV1_coverage`, because that is our coverage variable

## Step 1: keep the country-years we need




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
asia2000 <- subset(measles, region == "Asia" & year >= 2000)
```
:::




## Check the subset




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
head(asia2000)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 6 × 9
  iso3c  year measles_cases population MCV1_coverage MCV2_coverage country    
  <chr> <dbl>         <dbl>      <dbl>         <dbl>         <dbl> <chr>      
1 AFG    2023          2792   42239854            NA            NA Afghanistan
2 AFG    2022          5166   41128771            68            49 Afghanistan
3 AFG    2021          2900   40099462            63            44 Afghanistan
4 AFG    2020           640   38972230            66            43 Afghanistan
5 AFG    2019           353   37769499            64            41 Afghanistan
6 AFG    2018          2012   36686784            71            49 Afghanistan
# ℹ 2 more variables: region <chr>, sub_region <chr>
```


:::
:::




## Check the subset




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
summary(asia2000)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
       iso3c           year      measles_cases      population       
 Length   :1200   Min.   :2000   Min.   :     0   Min.   :2.825e+05  
 N.unique :  50   1st Qu.:2006   1st Qu.:     6   1st Qu.:3.857e+06  
 N.blank  :   0   Median :2012   Median :    94   Median :1.173e+07  
 Min.nchar:   3   Mean   :2012   Mean   :  3457   Mean   :8.494e+07  
 Max.nchar:   3   3rd Qu.:2017   3rd Qu.:  1223   3rd Qu.:4.811e+07  
                  Max.   :2023   Max.   :131441   Max.   :1.429e+09  
                                 NAs    :93                          
 MCV1_coverage   MCV2_coverage        country           region    
 Min.   : 0.00   Min.   : 0.00   Length   :1200   Length   :1200  
 1st Qu.:83.00   1st Qu.:80.75   N.unique :  50   N.unique :   1  
 Median :95.00   Median :94.00   N.blank  :   0   N.blank  :   0  
 Mean   :88.94   Mean   :84.80   Min.nchar:   4   Min.nchar:   4  
 3rd Qu.:98.00   3rd Qu.:98.00   Max.nchar:  38   Max.nchar:   4  
 Max.   :99.00   Max.   :99.00                                    
 NAs    :98      NAs    :288                                      
     sub_region  
 Length   :1200  
 N.unique :   5  
 N.blank  :   0  
 Min.nchar:  12  
 Max.nchar:  18  
                 
                 
```


:::
:::




## Step 2: calculate incidence

::: {.incremental}
* Raw case counts are useful, but they do not account for population size. Any ideas?
* cases per 100,000 people
:::

. . .




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
asia2000$cases_per_100k <- asia2000$measles_cases / asia2000$population * 100000
```
:::




## Check the calculation




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
summary(asia2000)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
       iso3c           year      measles_cases      population       
 Length   :1200   Min.   :2000   Min.   :     0   Min.   :2.825e+05  
 N.unique :  50   1st Qu.:2006   1st Qu.:     6   1st Qu.:3.857e+06  
 N.blank  :   0   Median :2012   Median :    94   Median :1.173e+07  
 Min.nchar:   3   Mean   :2012   Mean   :  3457   Mean   :8.494e+07  
 Max.nchar:   3   3rd Qu.:2017   3rd Qu.:  1223   3rd Qu.:4.811e+07  
                  Max.   :2023   Max.   :131441   Max.   :1.429e+09  
                                 NAs    :93                          
 MCV1_coverage   MCV2_coverage        country           region    
 Min.   : 0.00   Min.   : 0.00   Length   :1200   Length   :1200  
 1st Qu.:83.00   1st Qu.:80.75   N.unique :  50   N.unique :   1  
 Median :95.00   Median :94.00   N.blank  :   0   N.blank  :   0  
 Mean   :88.94   Mean   :84.80   Min.nchar:   4   Min.nchar:   4  
 3rd Qu.:98.00   3rd Qu.:98.00   Max.nchar:  38   Max.nchar:   4  
 Max.   :99.00   Max.   :99.00                                    
 NAs    :98      NAs    :288                                      
     sub_region   cases_per_100k     
 Length   :1200   Min.   :  0.00000  
 N.unique :   5   1st Qu.:  0.09461  
 N.blank  :   0   Median :  0.75410  
 Min.nchar:  12   Mean   :  8.31756  
 Max.nchar:  18   3rd Qu.:  3.81598  
                  Max.   :999.25567  
                  NAs    :93         
```


:::
:::




## Step 3: lowest coverage

Now we need to find the row with the lowest MCV1 coverage.

`which.min()` gives us the row number for the smallest value in a vector.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
lowest_coverage_row <- asia2000[which.min(asia2000$MCV1_coverage), c(
  "country",
  "year",
  "MCV1_coverage",
  "measles_cases",
  "population",
  "cases_per_100k"
)]

lowest_coverage_row
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 1 × 6
  country             year MCV1_coverage measles_cases population cases_per_100k
  <chr>              <dbl>         <dbl>         <dbl>      <dbl>          <dbl>
1 Korea, Democratic…  2022             0             0   26069416              0
```


:::
:::




## What did `which.min()` do?

::: {.incremental}
* `asia2000$MCV1_coverage` is one long vector of coverage values.
* `which.min(asia2000$MCV1_coverage)` finds the position of the smallest non-missing value.
* We use that position to pull out the whole row.
:::

## Step 4: highest incidence

Now we do the same thing for measles incidence.

This time we want the largest value, so we use `which.max()`.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
highest_incidence_row <- asia2000[which.max(asia2000$cases_per_100k), c(
  "country",
  "year",
  "MCV1_coverage",
  "measles_cases",
  "population",
  "cases_per_100k"
)]

highest_incidence_row
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 1 × 6
  country   year MCV1_coverage measles_cases population cases_per_100k
  <chr>    <dbl>         <dbl>         <dbl>      <dbl>          <dbl>
1 Mongolia  2016            98         30273    3029555           999.
```


:::
:::




## Step 5: are they the same country?

We now have two rows:

1. the row with the lowest MCV1 coverage
1. the row with the highest measles incidence per capita

The research question asks whether the **country** is the same.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
lowest_coverage_country <- lowest_coverage_row$country
highest_incidence_country <- highest_incidence_row$country

same_country <- lowest_coverage_country == highest_incidence_country

lowest_coverage_country
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Korea, Democratic People's Republic of"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
highest_incidence_country
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Mongolia"
```


:::
:::




## Step 6: write the answer with `if` {.scrollable}

This is a different kind of conditional.

Here we are not labeling every row. We are asking one yes/no question and then
choosing one sentence.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
if (same_country) {
  paste0(
    "Yes. ",
    lowest_coverage_country,
    " had both the lowest MCV1 coverage and the highest measles incidence ",
    "per capita."
  )
} else {
  paste0(
    "No. ",
    lowest_coverage_country,
    " had the lowest MCV1 coverage, but ",
    highest_incidence_country,
    " had the highest measles incidence per capita."
  )
}
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "No. Korea, Democratic People's Republic of had the lowest MCV1 coverage, but Mongolia had the highest measles incidence per capita."
```


:::
:::




## Make a small table




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
answer_table <- data.frame(
  statistic = c("Lowest MCV1 coverage", "Highest measles incidence"),
  country = c(lowest_coverage_row$country, highest_incidence_row$country),
  year = c(lowest_coverage_row$year, highest_incidence_row$year),
  MCV1_coverage = c(
    lowest_coverage_row$MCV1_coverage,
    highest_incidence_row$MCV1_coverage
  ),
  cases_per_100k = c(
    lowest_coverage_row$cases_per_100k,
    highest_incidence_row$cases_per_100k
  )
)

answer_table
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                  statistic                                country year
1      Lowest MCV1 coverage Korea, Democratic People's Republic of 2022
2 Highest measles incidence                               Mongolia 2016
  MCV1_coverage cases_per_100k
1             0         0.0000
2            98       999.2557
```


:::
:::




## Make a quick figure {.scrollable}




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot_rows <- !is.na(asia2000$MCV1_coverage) &
  !is.na(asia2000$cases_per_100k)

plot(
  asia2000$MCV1_coverage[plot_rows],
  asia2000$cases_per_100k[plot_rows],
  pch = 16,
  col = "gray70",
  xlab = "MCV1 coverage (%)",
  ylab = "Measles cases per 100,000 people",
  main = "Asian country-years since 2000"
)

points(
  lowest_coverage_row$MCV1_coverage,
  lowest_coverage_row$cases_per_100k,
  pch = 19,
  col = "red",
  cex = 1.5
)

points(
  highest_incidence_row$MCV1_coverage,
  highest_incidence_row$cases_per_100k,
  pch = 19,
  col = "blue",
  cex = 1.5
)

legend(
  "topright",
  legend = c("Lowest MCV1 coverage", "Highest incidence"),
  pch = 19,
  col = c("red", "blue"),
  bty = "n"
)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Walkthrough1_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::




## You try it!

Change the question:

> Since 2010, did the country in the Americas with the lowest MCV2 coverage also
> have the highest measles incidence per capita?

Write the code before looking at the solution.

## You try it!: hint

You need to change four pieces:

1. the region
1. the start year
1. the coverage variable
1. the object names, so you do not overwrite the first answer

## You try it!: solution




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
americas2010 <- subset(measles, region == "Americas" & year >= 2010)

americas2010$cases_per_100k <- americas2010$measles_cases /
  americas2010$population * 100000
```
:::




## You try it!: solution rows




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
lowest_mcv2_row <- americas2010[which.min(americas2010$MCV2_coverage), c(
  "country",
  "year",
  "MCV2_coverage",
  "measles_cases",
  "population",
  "cases_per_100k"
)]

highest_americas_incidence_row <- americas2010[
  which.max(americas2010$cases_per_100k),
  c(
    "country",
    "year",
    "MCV2_coverage",
    "measles_cases",
    "population",
    "cases_per_100k"
  )
]

lowest_mcv2_row$cases_per_100k <- round(
  lowest_mcv2_row$cases_per_100k,
  2
)

highest_americas_incidence_row$cases_per_100k <- round(
  highest_americas_incidence_row$cases_per_100k,
  2
)

lowest_mcv2_row
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 1 × 6
  country             year MCV2_coverage measles_cases population cases_per_100k
  <chr>              <dbl>         <dbl>         <dbl>      <dbl>          <dbl>
1 Venezuela, Boliva…  2019            13           548   28971683           1.89
```


:::
:::




## You try it!: solution rows, continued




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
highest_americas_incidence_row
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 1 × 6
  country             year MCV2_coverage measles_cases population cases_per_100k
  <chr>              <dbl>         <dbl>         <dbl>      <dbl>          <dbl>
1 Venezuela, Boliva…  2018            39          5667   29825653             19
```


:::
:::




## You try it!: compare them




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
lowest_mcv2_row$country
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
highest_americas_incidence_row$country
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Venezuela, Bolivarian Republic of"
```


:::
:::




## What did we practice?

- Starting with a question that a reader can understand
- Filtering rows with a logical condition
- Creating an incidence rate
- Finding minimum and maximum rows with `which.min()` and `which.max()`
- Using `if` and `else` to turn a comparison into a sentence
- Creating a table, figure, and short manuscript-style interpretation

Next time, we will repeat ideas like this across many countries.

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
